{"type":"Transition","startVersion":{"querySet":11,"identity":0,"ts":"cmnGvujOoxg="},"endVersion":{"querySet":12,"identity":0,"ts":"cmnGvujOoxg="},"modifications":[{"type":"QueryUpdated","queryId":11,"value":[{"_creationTime":1775448204023.2495,"_id":"js7ecby5njkwrygfwq54rqjgjh84ae3d","content":"Identify the claims related to vaccine misinformation that you identify from the discourse","createdAt":"2026-04-06T09:33:24.023+05:30","role":"user","threadId":"k57ezyc6ksz39va4d60t7tbxns84a35c","updatedAt":"2026-04-06T09:33:24.023+05:30"},{"_creationTime":1775448324572.4624,"_id":"js73q47w9k67z5kqgmtpfeg4md84asgk","content":"Based on the case study’s 227 posts (YouTube, Twitter, Reddit; Dec 1, 2025–Feb 15, 2026), claims analysis of a 30-post cross-platform sample surfaces several recurring vaccine-misinformation claim threads in the discourse (often framed around CDC guidance and “hidden” risks):

- “Vaccines cause autism” / “vaccines cannot be ruled out as a cause of autism.”  
  This appears repeatedly as a core contested claim—sometimes asserted directly, and often discussed via allegations that CDC language implies uncertainty.

- “The CDC changed/rewrote its vaccines-and-autism webpage,” framed as the CDC “softening” prior clarity.  
  A common narrative is that CDC site wording changed in a way that undermines the long-standing “no link” message.

- “There’s no proof vaccines don’t cause autism,” attributed to RFK Jr. (as reported/discussed in the discourse).  
  This shows up as an influential talking point that other users either amplify or rebut.

- “Hepatitis B vaccine at birth is unnecessary / should be delayed,” linked to claims about transmission being mainly through sex or injection drug use.  
  This is often part of a broader argument that newborn vaccination is overreach.

- “Hepatitis B vaccine (or childhood vaccines) weren’t properly tested,” including claims about lack of adequate placebo-controlled trials.  
  This theme is used to argue that safety assurances are not credible.

- “Hepatitis B vaccination causes serious harms (e.g., multiple sclerosis),” or that there’s a “signal” of severe outcomes.  
  This type of claim appears as part of a safety-scare cluster around routine vaccination.

- “COVID-19 vaccines are extremely dangerous / ‘most dangerous of all time’” and “COVID shots are killing children (more than reported).”  
  In the sample, some posts are promotional/opinion-heavy and others are too vague to verify cleanly, but the underlying misinformation claim is present as a discourse motif.

- “Vaccines spread disease via ‘shedding’,” used as an argument that vaccinated people can harm others.  
  This shows up as a recurring misinformation trope referenced in sourced rebuttals.

- “CDC/health agencies are hiding or manipulating safety data,” including claims implying corrupted decision-making (e.g., captured by politics/pharma).  
  Even when not stated as a single precise fact, it appears as a narrative claim driving distrust.

Net takeaway: the discourse clusters most strongly around (1) autism causation/CDC messaging, (2) newborn hepatitis B policy as a flashpoint for broader “unsafe/untested” claims, and (3) generalized COVID-19 vaccine harm posts."},{"insightType":"crossPlatform","question":"How does the autism/CDC-webpage narrative differ across YouTube vs X vs Reddit in sentiment and framing over time","whyThisHelps":"Shows where narratives originate, amplify, or shift tone across platforms."},{"insightType":"overview","question":"Outside the autism and Hep B themes, what other misinformation claim themes appear most on the case study’s 227 posts (YouTube, Twitter, Reddit; Dec 1, 2025–Feb 15, 2026), claims analysis of a 30-post cross-platform sample surfaces several recurring vaccine-misinformation claim threads in the discourse (often framed around CDC guidance and “hidden” risks):

- “Vaccines cause autism” / “vaccines cannot be ruled out as a cause of autism.”  
  This appears repeatedly as a core contested claim—sometimes asserted directly, and often discussed via allegations that CDC language implies uncertainty.

- “The CDC changed/rewrote its vaccines-and-autism webpage,” framed as the CDC “softening” prior clarity.  
  A common narrative is that CDC site wording changed in a way that undermines the long-standing “no link” message.

- “There’s no proof vaccines don’t cause autism,” attributed to RFK Jr. (as reported/discussed in the discourse).  
  This shows up as an influential talking point that other users either amplify or rebut.

- “Hepatitis B vaccine at birth is unnecessary / should be delayed,” linked to claims about transmission being mainly through sex or injection drug use.  
  This is often part of a broader argument that newborn vaccination is overreach.

- “Hepatitis B vaccine (or childhood vaccines) weren’t properly tested,” including claims about lack of adequate placebo-controlled trials.  
  This theme is used to argue that safety assurances are not credible.

- “Hepatitis B vaccination causes serious harms (e.g., multiple sclerosis),” or that there’s a “signal” of severe outcomes.  
  This type of claim appears as part of a safety-scare cluster around routine vaccination.

- “COVID-19 vaccines are extremely dangerous / ‘most dangerous of all time’” and “COVID shots are killing children (more than reported).”  
  In the sample, some posts are promotional/opinion-heavy and others are too vague to verify cleanly, but the underlying misinformation claim is present as a discourse motif.

- “Vaccines spread disease via ‘shedding’,” used as an argument that vaccinated people can harm others.  
  This shows up as a recurring misinformation trope referenced in sourced rebuttals.

- “CDC/health agencies are hiding or manipulating safety data,” including claims implying corrupted decision-making (e.g., captured by politics/pharma).  
  Even when not stated as a single precise fact, it appears as a narrative claim driving distrust.

Net takeaway: the discourse clusters most strongly around (1) autism causation/CDC messaging, (2) newborn hepatitis B policy as a flashpoint for broader “unsafe/untested” claims, and (3) generalized COVID-19 vaccine harm narratives.","type":"text"},{"result":{"result":{"__render":true,"mode":"provided_posts","partial":false,"posts":[{"actor":"cheryl ferenna","author":"cheryl ferenna","claims":[{"claimDate":"2025-12-01","claimId":1.0,"claimSummary":"The claim says the YouTube short movie by actor Cheryl Ferenna is an adaptation of a true story about COVID-19 vaccine misinformation from about four years ago. Some retrieved news/science items reference misinformation broadly (e.g., a lawfare piece about an Academies report on misinformation about science via Misinformation Studies Meets the Raw Milk Renaissance), but they do not address the film’s adaptation status. Kennedy Jr.’s role in broader vaccine-policy shifts, but still tying the core allegation to edits on the autism/vaccines page (e.g., [https://www.beaconjournal.com/story/opinion/columns/guest/2025/12/03/autism-and-vaccines-use-facts-not-fear-to-guide-choices-opinion/87505897007/](https://www.beaconjournal.com/story/opinion/columns/guest/2025/12/03/autism-and-vaccines-use-facts-not-fear-to-guide-choices-opinion/87505897007/)). Kennedy Jr.’s influence and as undermining prior messaging about vaccine safety and autism (e.g., [beaconjournal.com](https://www.beaconjournal.com/story/opinion/columns/guest/2025/12/27/robert-kennedy-ignores-what-science-tells-us-about-safety-of-vaccines-no-link-to-autism-opinion/87861749007/) and [theguardian.com](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2025/dec/05/autism-robert-f-kennedy-jr)). This is a warning sign for every healthcare system, including the NHS. I’m George, a UK pharmacist, and today we’re looking at what happens when politics begins shaping clinical messaging, and why this shift poses a real risk to public trust, vaccine uptake, and long-term health outcomes. In this video we cover: Why the CDC autism wording change matters Politics vs healthcare: when interests collide The US: a real-time example of politicised medicine UK pressure: is the NHS drifting the same way? How political messaging destroys trust What the NHS urgently needs to stay evidence-based How we protect patients in a political world Key message: When scientific clarity is diluted to satisfy political agendas, patients lose, the workforce loses, and years of progress can be undone overnight. Other outlets echo the same policy element, including a description that the panel’s new guidance says women who test negative can decide in consultation with a health care provider ([NBC Chicago](https://www.nbcchicago.com/news/national-international/cdc-advisory-panel-rolls-back-universal-hepatitis-b-vaccine-recommendation/3859100/), Today, and [19th News](https://19thnews.org/2025/12/vaccine-advisory-panel-ends-hepatitis-b-newborn/)). These sources align with the claim’s consultation-based phrasing, though some add timing details (e.g., suggestions about waiting until later) beyond the claim itself. No Wikipedia reference data was provided to contextualize the entities or policy language, and none of the retrieved sources are presented as verbatim committee wording—only summarized guidance.
